steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks for the function of the underlined sentence in the passage. The first two sentences establish that most Native languages belong to families, while a minority — Washoe and Chitimacha — are isolates with no known genealogical ties. The underlined sentence, introduced by "Yet," pivots to say those isolates might once have had family members that disappeared before the historical record began.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'a nonisolate language may have influenced both Washoe and Chitimacha to such a degree that their relationship to their former families is no longer discernible': The sentence says the other members of Washoe's and Chitimacha's families vanished — it does not say another language influenced or overwrote their classification. ✗
  • Choice B — 'the ancestral languages of both Washoe and Chitimacha were likely isolates that replaced nonisolate languages through geographic expansion': This reverses the direction. The sentence says extant nonisolate families may have expanded and displaced Washoe's and Chitimacha's relatives, not that Washoe and Chitimacha's ancestors displaced others. ✗
  • Choice C — 'the historical record tends to overrepresent nonisolate languages relative to isolates, such that the origins of Washoe and Chitimacha are obscure': The sentence attributes the loss of family members to their disappearance before the record began — not to bias in what the record chose to document. ✗
  • Choice D — 'the distinction made between present-day nonisolate languages and isolates like Washoe and Chitimacha may not have been applicable in the distant past': The sentence proposes that Washoe and Chitimacha could once have had relatives, meaning they were not always isolates — the isolate/nonisolate boundary may be a modern artifact, not a timeless one. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
